title: Daybreak
html:
Dawn came the way it does in dense forest: not as light but as a reduction in dark. The black between the trees went charcoal, then grey, then pale grey with shapes in it. The birds did not resume immediately. They waited until the light was certain before they committed to it.

I noted the delay. About 20 minutes between first light and first birdsong. Whatever had moved through the forest at night was still close enough at dawn that the birds were not willing to announce themselves.

I stayed at the overhang opening and did not move.

The fire had died to coals sometime in the early hours. Pita had fed it once more before she slept and then let it go out around what I estimated was 2 AM. The coals were cold by now, a pale grey mound at the back of the overhang, and the air under the rock smelled of wood smoke in a way that would carry if anything was searching by scent.

I filed that as tomorrow's problem and focused on the trees.

Nothing moved in the opening of the forest for the full 20 minutes of bird-delay. Then the birds started and the forest sounds came back in layers and the light got real. I let my shoulders drop half an inch.

Pita woke at the same moment the birds did. Not startled. She came up from sleep the way she had gone into it, in a single controlled motion, and looked at me, and I shook my head once: nothing. She nodded and looked at the treeline herself.

Maya woke 10 minutes later with her notebook already in her hand. I did not ask if she had slept.

Soo-Yun slept until the smell of the dead fire reached her, then sat up and looked around with the focused expression of someone checking that the world was still the world they fell asleep in.

It was. More or less.

The system timer when I checked it read 33:12:08. Which meant the dawn here ran on a roughly 15-hour night cycle, or the tutorial time was not synchronized with a real planet's rotation. I had no way to tell the difference and it did not change anything I needed to do today.

I divided the two dried rations into four shares. No one commented on the math. Soo-Yun had two crackers left from her packet and ate one of them instead of taking a share of the ration. I let her.

Pita ate her share in four bites and drank from her flask.

"We should move before midday," she said.

"Where to?" Maya asked.

Pita looked at me.

I thought about it. We had a position, water access, one cache found. The tutorial ran another 33 hours. Staying meant defending a fixed position for the remainder, which was manageable but static. Moving meant exposure and the chance of more caches, more information, contact with whatever was left of the other participants.

The loud man's group was still a variable I had not resolved.

"Northwest first," I said. "Then we decide."

Pita's expression did not change. She had already known that was coming.

We found the loud man's camp at the 30-minute mark. Not by searching. By smell.

Two fires, both burned down to ash. The ash was cold. They had been burning for hours before they went out. The site was a rough clearing between three large trees, the ground scuffed and compressed from a lot of feet, and there were 7 people still there.

The loud man was one of them. He was sitting against the base of one of the trees with his knees up and a piece of rope wrapped around one forearm that someone had used as a pressure bandage. His face had the specific flatness of someone who has just finished being afraid and has not yet decided what to feel next.

The other 6 were in various states. Two of them had injuries I could see. One woman was unconscious or asleep against a root. The rest were awake and looking at us with the expression of people who needed something but had stopped expecting to get it.

I counted. 7 here, 84 remaining per the last update. That meant 77 were elsewhere, in other groups or alone.

The loud man looked up when we stopped at the clearing edge. He looked at me specifically.

"You're the one from the kappa," he said. His voice was lower than I remembered it.

"Yes."

He looked at the machete. Then at Pita. Then at Soo-Yun.

"They came at the fires," he said. "Three of them. Not kappas. Something else."

"How many did you lose?"

His jaw moved. 'Four. Two ran. We don't know where."'), p("So 6 from his group accounted for in the 19 gone by hour 10. The other 13 from other sources.

Maya had her notebook out. She was writing without looking at it, eyes on the camp.

I looked at the unconscious woman by the root. Her chest was moving. Breathing fine. Exhaustion or a hit to the head.

"What came for you?" Pita asked. She was looking at the man with the bandaged arm.

He held up his forearm. Through the rope bandage, dark staining. A bite. The shape of the wound through the fabric was wrong for a human mouth and too large for most animals I knew.

"Tall," the loud man said. "Dark. Moved fast. When you hit them they felt it but it didn't stop them." He paused. "Fire slowed them down. They didn't like the light directly. They circled."

So the nocturnal hunters were photosensitive but not stopped by fire. They would approach to the edge of the light and wait. A camp with two fires would have a ring of darkness between the fires and the treeline, and anything patient enough to find that ring could come through it.

That was a design note. Whoever built this tutorial had thought about the geometry of fear.

I looked at the 7 survivors and then at the trees around the clearing.

"Can you move?" I asked the loud man.

He looked at me steadily. 'My name is Dae-Jung.'

I noted the correction. 'Can you move, Dae-Jung.'

"Yes." He stood, slower than he would have yesterday, but steady. "Most of us can."

The woman by the root opened her eyes at the sound of movement. She sat up and looked at her hands and then at us. No panic. She had been awake.

'Seven of them, four of us,' I thought. 'The math on that is not straightforward.'

But Dae-Jung had taken a bite and stayed. He had kept 6 people alive through the night against something that had killed 4. That was information.

"There is a defensible position 30 minutes southeast," I said. "Rock face. One approach. If you want to move, we can take you there."

Dae-Jung looked at his 6 people. Some of them were already starting to gather things.

"We want to move," he said.

The walk back was slower with 11 people, two of them impaired. Pita ranged ahead of the group and behind it in turns, checking the treeline. She did not check in with me when she did this. She did not need to.

Soo-Yun walked beside the woman who had been unconscious, whose name turned out to be Renata, from a cold country in the north whose descriptor I filed as snow country for now. Renata had taken a blow to the head from a tree branch during the chaos of the attack, not from the creatures. She had a bruise above her right ear and a headache she mentioned once and then did not mention again.

Soo-Yun did not say much to her. She matched her pace and stayed beside her and once when Renata's footing went wrong on a root, she caught her arm without making it into something. Renata looked at her, and Soo-Yun looked ahead, and they kept walking.

I watched this from two meters back and said nothing.

Dae-Jung walked near me. Not beside me. Near. The difference was that he was not asking for something. He was deciding whether to.

Around the 20-minute mark he decided.

"How did you know not to light a fire?"

"The tutorial tip said purification was recommended," I said. "If the system is recommending basic survival steps, the environment punishes skipping them. Fire is the same category."

He was quiet for a moment. 'That's a cold way to read it.'

"It's the accurate way."

He did not argue with that. He looked at the machete again.

"I don't have a class yet either," he said.

I looked at him. He met it.

"The system told me when we entered the forest," he said. "No class assigned. I checked twice."

Two of us with no class. I thought about what the kappas and the nocturnal hunters had in common with subduing a group that panicked and screaming at ceilings and staying alive through a bad night, and I could not find the pattern that should have triggered a class assignment in either of us. Whatever the condition was, it was not simply surviving.

I filed it and kept moving.

The group was settling at the rock face when it happened.

I was at the clearing edge doing a final sweep of the approach when the system fired without warning, no preamble, no lead notification.

---

【"Class Assignment: Arbiter"】

【"You have demonstrated sustained threat assessment, resource allocation, and group coordination under adversarial conditions without recourse to direct authority."】

【"Primary Attribute: Perception. Secondary: Intelligence."】

【"Passive: Read the Room -- Enhanced environmental and social threat detection. Scales with Perception."】

【"Active: Weight the Odds -- Once per hour, analyze a target entity or situation for exploitable variables. Duration: 30 seconds. Cooldown: 60 minutes."】

【"Arbiter is a rare classification. 3 known Arbiters in current tutorial instance."】

---

I read it twice.

Arbiter. Not a fighter class, not a scout class. A reading class. The passive ability was called Read the Room.

I almost smiled at that. Almost.

3 known Arbiters in the instance. Which meant 2 others somewhere in this forest had just received the same notification, and the system had told all three of us simultaneously that the others existed. That was either an invitation or a warning, and I was not sure the distinction mattered yet.

I closed the notification and looked at the 11 people settling around the overhang.

Maya was directing the injured man to sit with his back against the rock, which gave her a look at his bandaged arm while seeming to just be solving the seating problem. She had not been asked to do this. She had seen the geometry of it and moved.

Pita was at the far end of the clearing, crouched at the sight line, watching northwest.

Soo-Yun had produced the crackers again and was offering the last of them to Renata, who refused twice before Soo-Yun set them on the ground beside her and walked away, which meant Renata would eat them without having to accept them.

Dae-Jung was looking at me from across the clearing.

He had seen the notification flicker. He could not read it but he had seen my eyes track something.

I walked over to him.

"Class?" he asked quietly.

"Yes."

"Good class?"

I thought about Read the Room and Weight the Odds and a rare classification shared by 3 people in a forest full of things that killed 19 participants overnight.

"Useful," I said.

He nodded. He did not ask what it was. I noted that.

---

【"Tutorial Chapter 1: Hour 15 Update"】

【"Participants Remaining: 79 / 103"】

【"Tip: Groups improve survivability. So does knowing when to leave one."】

---

79 now. Down 5 more since the 10-hour mark. Morning had been quieter than the night but not safe.

I looked at 11 people around a rock face with 33 hours left on the clock and thought about the tip: groups improve survivability, and knowing when to leave one.

The system was not reassuring anyone. It was noting that both things were true at the same time.

'I know,' I thought. 'I already know.'

I found a position at the overhang edge with a clear line to the approach and sat down and pulled up the new class tab and read it again, more carefully this time, looking for the edges of what it could and could not do.

33 hours. 79 people.

The tutorial was still going.
